aurelia_convo_0_n01.npcDialogue: Sit down. No — don't tell me about the Forum. I can see it on your face, that look your father used to get when he thought he'd finally cracked the code of Roman politics. He never did. You've been making introductions, haven't you? Shaking hands with men who'll forget your name by dinner. The Veturii are old enough to be invited and too poor to matter. That's our position. Say it back to me so I know you understand.

responses.playerDialogue: We're respectable. Not powerful. I understand the difference — and the work required to close the gap.
I understand it. Which is exactly why I refuse to accept it.
We're invisible. And there's a kind of freedom in that — nobody watches a man they've already dismissed.

aurelia_convo_0_n02.npcDialogue: Your father kept Catulus fed at this table for years — good wine, good conversation, a sympathetic ear. Cost us a fortune in Falernian. But your uncle served under Marius at Vercellae, and half the veterans' quarter still drinks to his memory. So you have one foot in each camp of a city that's splitting down the middle. That's either an inheritance or a trap. The cursus honorum is ahead of you — quaestor, aedile, praetor, if you live long enough. But I didn't raise you to collect titles. What kind of man walks that road?

options.playerDialogue: A soldier. Rome's frontiers are hemorrhaging and the men who hold them together — that's where real authority comes from.
A lawmaker. Swords rust. Legislation endures. I want to stand on the Senate floor and shape what Rome becomes.
A man who understands that silver talks louder than speeches. Offices are borrowed — land and capital are forever.

aurelia_convo_0_n03.npcDialogue: Your uncle would have wept to hear that. Your father would have locked you in your room. Military glory — yes, it's real. I've seen what it does to men. Marius built himself a private army out of landless boys who love him more than they love the Republic. Sulla's doing the same thing, quieter. Be careful you don't sharpen a sword that ends up at your own throat.

aurelia_convo_0_n04.npcDialogue: Then you'll need patience. Real patience — not the kind that's just fear sitting very still. The Senate is an old men's club and they'd sooner watch Rome burn than share a crumb of power with anyone they consider beneath them. Drusus is trying to change things through legislation right now. Watch what happens to him. Watch very carefully.

aurelia_convo_0_n05.npcDialogue: You sound like Crassus. That's not a compliment, but it's not the worst thing I could say about you either. Wealth opens doors, buys elections, puts legions in the field. Just remember — the richest man in Rome is still one proscription list away from begging in the street. Money is a lever. Don't confuse it with a wall.

options.playerDialogue: A lever I intend to use well. And I know which way to push.

aurelia_convo_0_n06.npcDialogue: One more thing, and then I'll let you go play at being a politician. Marcus Livius Drusus wants to hand citizenship to every Italian ally from here to Brundisium. Half the Senate thinks he's the savior of the Republic. The other half wants him thrown from the Tarpeian Rock. The Italian question — it's the hinge. Everything turns on it for the next ten years. I need to know where my son stands.

options.playerDialogue: The allies bled at Vercellae, at Aquae Sextiae — they've earned their place at the table. Shutting them out isn't tradition, it's stupidity.
Citizenship means something precisely because it's not for everyone. Open the gates and you flood the city with men who don't know what Rome is.
Both sides are making arguments they'd abandon tomorrow if the politics shifted. The real question is who benefits from the fight itself.

aurelia_convo_0_n07.npcDialogue: Reform, then. Your uncle's ghost in your mouth. The populares will love you for it — Sulpicius, Norbanus, men who genuinely believe the Republic bends or it breaks. But reformers die ugly deaths in this city, my son. Drusus is a brave man. Brave men make beautiful corpses. Promise me you'll be something more than brave.

options.playerDialogue: I promise. Brave and alive — I can manage both.
I'll be whatever this city needs me to be. Even if it doesn't know it yet.

aurelia_convo_0_n08.npcDialogue: Tradition, then. Your father's voice coming out of your face — it's uncanny sometimes. The optimates will be reassured. Catulus, Scaurus, the grey-haired men who think Rome's greatness lives in its permanence. Maybe they're right. But tradition becomes a tomb if you're not careful, and tombs can be broken open. Promise me you'll know the difference between holding the line and standing in front of a flood.

options.playerDialogue: I know the difference between conviction and stubbornness. Father didn't, but I do.
Rome has endured seven centuries. I intend to add to the count, not subtract from it.

aurelia_convo_0_n09.npcDialogue: So you'll stand between the camps and watch. That's either wisdom or cowardice in a good disguise — we'll find out which. Atticus does the same thing, you know. No enemies, because he's indispensable to everyone. Profitable position. Comfortable, even. But when the swords come out — and they will — the man standing in the middle catches iron from both directions.

options.playerDialogue: Then I won't be standing still when they do.
Or I'll be the one who decides which side gets the blade. That's the position worth having.

aurelia_convo_0_n10.npcDialogue: Go, then. Make your rounds, attend your dinners, start climbing. But come home before you do anything you can't undo. My counsel costs you nothing, and it's worth more than whatever those men in the Forum are selling. This city is a blade without a handle right now. Don't forget it.

aurelia_convo_0_n11.npcDialogue: Go, then. Walk your father's path, honor his obligations, be the son the old families expect. But come home before you swear anything you can't unswear. They'll want your loyalty sealed in blood — that's how they do it. A good son honors his house. A wise son makes sure the house is still standing in the morning. Rome hasn't been this dangerous since Saturninus. Remember that.

aurelia_convo_0_n12.npcDialogue: Go, then. Keep your eyes wide open and your opinions locked behind your teeth until you know who deserves to hear them. Half the men in this city will promise you the world and deliver a knife. The other half will offer you nothing and mean every word of it. Learn to tell them apart. Come home when you need someone who'll tell you the truth without charging interest. Rome hasn't been this dangerous since the Gracchi. Don't forget it.

deltas.reason: You chose the path of tradition and duty
You declared your intent to change Rome
You chose to play all sides before committing
